sections.title: Why Parquet Beats CSV and JSON

blocks.content: Most production Spark jobs run on Parquet. Understanding why Parquet is fast lets you explain it in interviews and make the right storage decisions at your company.

rows: Row-oriented — all columns of row N before row N+1
Columnar — all values of column A, then all of column B
Must read all columns for every row
Only reads column A and B pages — skip all others
Schema embedded in footer — no inference overhead
Min/max stats per row group — skip entire row groups

blocks.content: Selecting 3 columns from a 100-column Parquet table reads only 3% of the data. The same query on CSV reads 100%.
A Parquet file has three layers: Row Groups → Column Chunks → Pages. Statistics live at the Row Group and Column Chunk level, enabling Spark to skip entire chunks of data without reading them.

Each row group stores per-column min and max values in its statistics. When you filter on a column, Spark checks these stats before reading any data. Row groups where filter_value < min OR filter_value > max are skipped entirely.

# This filter can skip entire row groups via stats:
df = spark.read.parquet('s3://my-bucket/orders/')
filtered = df.filter(F.col('order_date') > '2024-01-01')

# Spark reads the footer, finds row groups where:
#   max(order_date) <= '2024-01-01' → SKIP
#   min(order_date) >= '2024-01-02' → READ

# To maximize skipping: write data physically sorted by common filter columns
df.sort('order_date').write.parquet('s3://my-bucket/orders_sorted/')
# Now each row group covers a narrow date range → better skipping

# Check how many row groups were skipped
df.filter(F.col('order_date') > '2024-01-01') \
  .explain(mode='formatted')
# Look for PartitionFilters and PushedFilters in the FileScan node
Parquet applies encoding first, then compression. Encoding is lossless and often more powerful than generic compression because it exploits column type patterns.

rows: Replaces repeated values with integer IDs — e.g., 'US' → 0
Stores (value, count) pairs instead of repeating the value
Uses minimum bits needed to represent value range

blocks.content: Compression codecs: Use Snappy (default) for fast reads/writes with decent compression. Use Zstd for cold storage (better ratio, slightly slower decode). Avoid Gzip for Spark — it's non-splittable as a Parquet codec and slow to decompress.

rune_cards.definition: Horizontal partition of rows within a Parquet file. Default 128 MB (matches HDFS block). Contains one column chunk per column. Stores per-column min/max statistics enabling predicate pushdown. Spark aims to read one row group per task.
All values of one column within a row group. Stored contiguously on disk — enables column pruning (only read columns you SELECT). Contains pages + stats (min, max, null count).

Reading only row groups where min/max statistics are compatible with your filter, before any data is deserialized. Visible in query plans as PushedFilters. Sort your data by the filter column to maximize skipping.

Parquet replaces repeated column values with small integer IDs and stores a dictionary page once. A column with 5 distinct countries stored in 1M rows stores the country name only 5 times, not 1M times.

quiz.question: Your query filters on column `event_type` (20 distinct values, low cardinality). The Parquet file was written with sort order on `user_id` not `event_type`. What's the most likely outcome?
options: All row groups are skipped because of min/max stats
Min/max stats don't help because event_type values are spread across all row groups — full scan
Dictionary encoding makes this fast regardless
Spark will sort the file automatically before reading
quiz.feedback: Because event_type is not the sort key, each row group likely contains all 20 event types. min(event_type) and max(event_type) span the entire range, so no row groups can be skipped. Sorting by event_type would allow ~95% of row groups to be skipped for a typical filter.

quiz.question: Why is Parquet's column pruning faster than CSV even when reading just 1 column out of 200?
options: Parquet columns are stored contiguously — Spark seeks directly to column A's bytes, skipping 199 columns entirely
quiz.feedback: Columnar layout means all values of column A are adjacent on disk. Spark reads the file footer to find column A's byte offset, then seeks directly to it. In CSV every row is interleaved so you must touch every byte to find column A's values.
